working-tree-staging-and-history.label: Working Tree, Staging Area, and History
working-tree-staging-and-history.summary: Follow one file from an ordinary edit through the staging area into a committed snapshot.

sketch1.title: The Role of the Staging Area

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Try it yourself: Explore the Working Tree
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt:
Right after the sketch "The Working Tree Explained", practice that exact idea with this task. Modify a file in your working tree and check its status. Create a file named `example.txt`, add some text, and use `git status` to see its state.

This practice opens in a fresh workspace. First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter to prepare the local repository. Then type the requested Git command and press Enter. Produce the result for explore the Working Tree so it reinforces Working Tree, Staging Area, and History.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.instructions:
Modify a file in your working tree and check its status. Create a file named `example.txt`, add some text, and use `git status` to see its state.

This practice opens in a fresh workspace. First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter to prepare the local repository. Then type the requested Git command and press Enter.

main_sh.content:
# First type: bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh
# Press Enter, then type the Git command requested in the prompt.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
git init -q -b main
git config user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
printf '.zoeskoul/
main.sh
' >> .git/info/exclude
printf 'Project baseline\n' > example.txt
git add example.txt
git commit -q -m "Add project baseline"
printf 'Working tree update\n' >> example.txt

main.sh
' >> .git/info/exclude
printf 'Project baseline\n' > example.txt
git add example.txt

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Try it yourself: Use Git Status to Verify Changes
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt:
Right after the sketch "Git Status as a Bridge", practice that exact idea with this task. Use `git status` to verify the current state of your repository after committing changes. Ensure the working tree is clean.

This practice opens in a fresh workspace. First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter to prepare the local repository. Then type the requested Git command and press Enter. Produce the result for use Git Status to Verify Changes so it reinforces Working Tree, Staging Area, and History.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Run `git status` to check the repository state.

help.concept: `git status` shows the current state of the working tree and staging area.
help.hint_1: After committing, `git status` should show a clean working tree.
help.hint_2: Ensure no files are staged or modified.

git commit -q -m "Add second project note"

single-choice-1.title: Identify the Working Tree
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the working tree in Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Think about where you make changes before staging them.

help.concept: The working tree is where you make changes to files before staging.
help.hint_1: It's the directory where your project files are located.
help.hint_2: It's not the staging area or commit history.

options.a: The directory where you make changes to files
options.b: The area where changes are staged for commit

working-tree-intro.title: The Working Tree Explained
working-tree-intro.bodyMarkdown:
The working tree is the directory where you make changes to your files. It's the place where you can freely edit, add, or remove files before deciding to track them with Git. When you modify a file, it exists in the working tree until you stage it for a commit.

Worked example: Imagine you have a project folder with a file named `notes.txt`. You open this file and add some new information. At this point, the changes are only in your working tree. You can check the status of your working tree using the command:

```bash
git status
```

This command will show you which files have been modified but not yet staged for commit. It's a crucial step to ensure you know what changes are pending before you move them to the staging area.

staging-area-intro.title: The Role of the Staging Area
staging-area-intro.bodyMarkdown:
The staging area, also known as the index, is a space where you prepare changes for the next commit. It acts as a buffer between the working tree and the commit history. You add files to the staging area using the `git add` command.

Worked example: Continuing with `notes.txt`, after editing, you decide to include these changes in your next commit. You stage the file by running:

```bash
git add notes.txt
```

Now, `notes.txt` is in the staging area. You can verify this by running `git status` again, which will show the file as staged for commit. This step allows you to selectively choose which changes to include in your next commit, providing flexibility and control over your project history.

commit-history-intro.title: The Importance of Commit History
commit-history-intro.bodyMarkdown:
Commit history is the record of snapshots of your project at various points in time. Each commit represents a state of the project, capturing the changes made since the last commit.

Worked example: Once `notes.txt` is staged, you can commit the changes with:

```bash
git commit -m "Add new information to notes"
```

This command creates a new commit in the history, with a message describing the changes. You can view the commit history using:

```bash
git log --oneline
```

This will show a list of commits, each with a unique identifier and message. The commit history is essential for tracking the evolution of your project and understanding what changes were made and why.

git-status-bridge.title: Git Status as a Bridge
git-status-bridge.bodyMarkdown:
`git status` is a powerful command that acts as a bridge between the working tree, staging area, and commit history. It provides a summary of the current state of your project, showing which files are modified, staged, or untracked.

Worked example: After making changes to multiple files, you can run:

```bash
git status
```

This command will display the status of each file, helping you decide what to stage or commit next. It's an essential tool for managing your workflow and ensuring that you don't accidentally commit unwanted changes.
